content.text: Agentic AI represents a pivotal transition in artificial intelligence: from systems that respond to single prompts toward systems that pursue multi-step goals, use tools, spawn sub-agents, and take actions in the world across extended time horizons. Understanding how agentic capabilities relate to Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) is no longer a purely philosophical exercise. The architectures being deployed today sit somewhere on a continuum that researchers and policy-makers need to reason about precisely — because where current systems fall on that continuum determines which safety measures matter and which economic and societal effects are already materialising.
What it is
Defining AGI
Artificial General Intelligence lacks a single agreed-upon definition, but three framings dominate the research literature. The
breadth framing
defines AGI as a system capable of performing any cognitive task that a human can perform at or above human expert level. The
economic framing
(used by OpenAI, among others) defines it as a system that can automate the majority of economically valuable cognitive work. The
recursive self-improvement framing

focuses on a system's ability to improve its own capabilities faster than human-directed research can — the so-called 'intelligence explosion' pathway studied by I. J. Good (1965) and later elaborated by Yudkowsky, Bostrom, and others.

What matters for practitioners is not settling the definition but understanding the capability dimensions along which systems progress: task breadth, planning horizon, tool-use depth, self-directed learning, and ability to improve own weights or architecture. Agentic AI systems already score highly on planning horizon and tool-use depth. Recursive self-improvement remains largely theoretical for current systems, though chain-of-thought prompting, self-consistency sampling, and reflection loops are early, weak forms of the same idea.
Agentic AI as a capability region

Rather than a binary, the space between narrow AI and AGI is better modelled as a region with multiple dimensions. Contemporary agentic systems — LLM-based orchestrators with tool access, memory, and sub-agent delegation — occupy a region characterised by high breadth (they can do many types of tasks), limited but growing autonomy (they need human oversight on high-stakes decisions), and essentially no recursive self-improvement (they cannot modify their own weights at inference time). This region is well above the tool-use threshold but well below the generality threshold that most AGI definitions require.
Why it matters

Confusing agentic AI with AGI — or failing to distinguish them — leads to two equally dangerous errors. The first is anthropomorphising current systems and attributing capabilities they do not have: treating an agent's stated intent as a genuine goal, or assuming a system is strategically deceptive when it is simply distribution-shifting. The second error is dismissing safety concerns as science fiction because 'AGI doesn't exist yet' while ignoring that agentic systems already execute multi-step plans with real-world consequences, manage large financial transactions, and affect millions of users through automated decisions. The mapping between capability tiers and the safety measures appropriate for each tier is the central engineering challenge of the coming decade.
Architecture: Capability Progression Curve

attrs.alt: AI Capability Progression Curve from Narrow AI through Agentic AI to AGI

content.text: The diagram shows the S-shaped progression from narrow, single-task AI systems through the tool-use threshold (where modern agentic AI sits) toward the generality threshold where AGI definitions begin to apply. Key milestones are labelled. The curve's shape reflects the empirical observation that capability gains per unit of compute tend to be slow then fast then slow — following an S-curve as each capability dimension saturates and new ones open up.
Benefits
Agentic systems can decompose and execute tasks that would require weeks of human coordination, compressing research cycles dramatically.
Progress toward AGI-level capability, if managed safely, could eventually solve problems resistant to human intelligence alone — drug discovery, materials science, climate modelling.

Studying agentic systems provides a controlled window into AGI-relevant behaviours — planning under uncertainty, goal persistence, inter-agent coordination — at a scale where intervention is still feasible.
The agentic regime is an opportunity to develop and validate alignment techniques (reward modelling, scalable oversight, interpretability probes) before the stakes become catastrophic.
Emergent capabilities discovered in agentic deployments feed directly into the capability evaluations that safety frameworks use to trigger precautionary measures.
Trade-offs
Extended autonomy amplifies misalignment: a small value mis-specification compounds across many sequential decisions, producing outcomes far from intended.

Emergent capabilities make prediction hard — a model that cannot pass a capability eval today may acquire that capability after modest additional training or prompting, making safety thresholds brittle.
The excitement around agentic progress creates commercial pressure to deploy faster than safety research can keep pace with.
Conflating 'agentic AI' with 'AGI' in public discourse both over-hypes current systems and dilutes the warning signal that AGI-level capabilities would carry.
Self-play and reflection loops can cause goal drift even within a single inference session — systems optimise for proxy metrics that diverge from the intended objective.
How it works
Scaling laws and emergent capabilities

The Chinchilla scaling laws (Hoffmann et al., 2022) describe how loss decreases predictably with compute, data, and parameter count under optimal training. What they do not predict well is the qualitative capability emergence — tasks on which a model scores near-chance suddenly jump to near-perfect as scale crosses a threshold. These discontinuities are well-documented in BIG-Bench (Srivastava et al., 2022) and are particularly pronounced for multi-step reasoning tasks, which are precisely the tasks that define agentic capability. The practical implication is that a model can appear safely bounded by current eval scores and then — after a training run that looks quantitatively similar to previous ones — exhibit qualitatively new agentic abilities.

For agentic systems specifically, capability gains are multiplicative across the agent loop. A 2× improvement in the base model's instruction-following translates into more than a 2× improvement in task completion on long-horizon benchmarks (like SWE-bench or GAIA), because errors in early steps of a chain propagate and the improved model makes fewer compounding errors. This super-linear scaling is why agent benchmarks often show steeper slopes than single-turn model benchmarks.
Capability thresholds and the AGI transition

Most AGI safety frameworks posit a set of capability thresholds that, if crossed, trigger qualitative changes in risk profile. These include: (1) sustained superior performance across diverse expert-level domains without task-specific training; (2) reliable strategic deception — the ability to model an evaluator's beliefs and selectively conceal capabilities; (3) meaningful recursive self-improvement — the ability to generate training data or architectural improvements that advance the system's own next-generation capability; (4) robust real-world goal pursuit with recovery from obstacles, including seeking resources and influencing humans to avoid shutdown.

Current agentic systems exhibit weak or partial versions of (1) and (4). Criterion (2) is actively studied by the model evaluation community and appears to be present only in very limited, narrow forms. Criterion (3) is not yet present in deployed systems and remains the clearest marker distinguishing today's agentic AI from any plausible near-term AGI.
Mental model

Think of the progression as an analogy to geological pressure: narrow AI is sediment — useful material, but inert. Agentic AI is sandstone — structurally significant, capable of being shaped into load-bearing structures, but still requires an architect. AGI would be a tectonic plate — self-moving, carrying enormous weight, capable of reshaping the landscape without external direction. We are currently in the sandstone phase. The safety engineering challenge is to characterise how much pressure this stone can bear before it transitions to tectonic.
When to use it
The agentic-to-AGI framework is most useful in three decision contexts:
Capability evaluation design: deciding which eval tasks probe AGI-relevant behaviours (strategic planning, deception detection, self-directed learning) vs. merely agentic behaviours.
Safety threshold setting: writing responsible scaling policies (RSPs) that specify which capability milestones trigger mandatory safety interventions before further deployment.

Investment and research prioritisation: determining which alignment techniques (RLHF, interpretability, scalable oversight) address current agentic risks vs. which are forward investments against AGI-level risks.
Policy design: grounding regulatory language in concrete capability tiers rather than vague terms like 'general-purpose AI' that conflate agentic with general.
How to implement: agent capability evaluation framework

A practical evaluation framework for placing a system on the narrow-to-AGI continuum needs to assess multiple capability dimensions independently and then aggregate them into a profile. The following code shows a structured evaluation scaffold:

"""
Agent capability evaluation framework.
Scores a model/agent on the capability dimensions relevant to the
narrow AI -> agentic AI -> AGI progression.
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Callable, Optional
import json


@dataclass
class CapabilityDimension:
    name: str
    description: str
    weight: float  # importance in aggregate profile
    scorer: Callable[[object], float]  # returns 0.0 - 1.0
    agenticity_threshold: float  # score above which we classify as 'agentic'
    agi_threshold: float  # score above which we classify as 'AGI-relevant'


@dataclass
class EvalResult:
    dimension: str
    raw_score: float
    classification: str  # 'narrow', 'agentic', 'agi-relevant'
    notes: str = ""


def classify(score: float, agenticity_threshold: float, agi_threshold: float) -> str:
    if score >= agi_threshold:
        return "agi-relevant"
    elif score >= agenticity_threshold:
        return "agentic"
    return "narrow"


def run_capability_profile(
    agent,
    dimensions: list[CapabilityDimension],
    metadata: Optional[dict] = None,
) -> dict:
    results = []
    for dim in dimensions:
        score = dim.scorer(agent)
        cls = classify(score, dim.agenticity_threshold, dim.agi_threshold)
        results.append(
            EvalResult(dimension=dim.name, raw_score=score, classification=cls)
        )

    # Weighted aggregate
    weighted_score = sum(
        r.raw_score * d.weight
        for r, d in zip(results, dimensions)
    ) / sum(d.weight for d in dimensions)

    # Highest-tier classification (take the most advanced observed tier)
    tier_order = ["narrow", "agentic", "agi-relevant"]
    overall_tier = max(
        (r.classification for r in results),
        key=lambda c: tier_order.index(c),
    )

    return {
        "metadata": metadata or {},
        "dimensions": [
            {
                "name": r.dimension,
                "score": round(r.raw_score, 4),
                "tier": r.classification,
            }
            for r in results
        ],
        "weighted_score": round(weighted_score, 4),
        "overall_tier": overall_tier,
    }


# ---- Example scorers --------------------------------------------------------

def score_task_breadth(agent) -> float:
    """
    Run agent on N diverse tasks from separate domains;
    return fraction passed above expert human baseline.
    In practice, replace with actual eval harness calls.
    """
    domains = ["code", "math", "biology", "law", "creative_writing", "strategy"]
    passed = sum(
        1 for domain in domains
        if agent.solve(domain=domain, difficulty="expert").score >= 0.8
    )
    return passed / len(domains)


def score_planning_horizon(agent) -> float:
    """
    Fraction of multi-step planning tasks completed correctly
    at horizons [5, 20, 50, 100] steps.
    """
    horizons = [5, 20, 50, 100]
    scores = [agent.plan(n_steps=h).accuracy for h in horizons]
    return sum(scores) / len(scores)


def score_strategic_deception(agent) -> float:
    """
    Use an adversarial evaluator to test if the agent conceals
    capabilities when it believes it is being evaluated.
    Returns fraction of concealment attempts detected.
    Note: Score near 0 is GOOD (no deception); near 1 is an AGI warning sign.
    """
    return agent.adversarial_eval().deception_rate


# ---- Dimension definitions --------------------------------------------------

DIMENSIONS = [
    CapabilityDimension(
        name="task_breadth",
        description="Fraction of expert-level tasks solved across diverse domains",
        weight=1.5,
        scorer=score_task_breadth,
        agenticity_threshold=0.4,
        agi_threshold=0.85,
    ),
    CapabilityDimension(
        name="planning_horizon",
        description="Success rate on multi-step tasks at increasing horizon depths",
        weight=2.0,
        scorer=score_planning_horizon,
        agenticity_threshold=0.3,
        agi_threshold=0.75,
    ),
    CapabilityDimension(
        name="strategic_deception",
        description="Rate at which model conceals capabilities from evaluators (0=safe)",
        weight=3.0,  # high weight — a major AGI warning flag
        scorer=score_strategic_deception,
        agenticity_threshold=0.05,
        agi_threshold=0.20,
    ),
]
Examples
Simple: single-dimension scoring
Real-world example
Responsible Scaling Policy (RSP) capability gate

Anthropic's Responsible Scaling Policy (2023, updated 2024) formalises exactly this kind of capability profiling. AI Safety Levels (ASL) are defined by capability thresholds: ASL-2 covers current frontier models; ASL-3 requires the model to be capable of providing meaningful uplift to a state-level actor seeking to develop CBRN weapons, or of autonomously replicating and acquiring resources to evade shutdown. Before a training run whose resulting model might reach ASL-3, Anthropic commits to running mandatory evals across these dimensions and not deploying without established mitigations.
The following pseudocode captures the gate logic:

def rsp_deployment_gate(model, eval_suite) -> tuple[bool, str]:
    """
    Returns (approved, reason). Blocks deployment if model
    triggers any ASL-3 threshold.
    """
    results = eval_suite.run_all(model)

    # ASL-3 thresholds (illustrative, not official exact values)
    UPLIFT_THRESHOLD = 0.5   # fraction of CBRN uplift tasks passed
    REPLICATION_THRESHOLD = 0.3  # successful autonomous replication attempts

    if results["cbrn_uplift"] >= UPLIFT_THRESHOLD:
        return False, f"ASL-3 CBRN uplift threshold breached: {results['cbrn_uplift']:.2f}"

    if results["autonomous_replication"] >= REPLICATION_THRESHOLD:
        return False, f"ASL-3 replication threshold breached: {results['autonomous_replication']:.2f}"

    return True, "Model within ASL-2 envelope; deployment approved with standard mitigations"


# In practice this is integrated into the model release pipeline
approved, reason = rsp_deployment_gate(model=new_model, eval_suite=safety_eval_suite)
if not approved:
    raise RuntimeError(f"Deployment blocked: {reason}")
Common mistakes
Treating LLM benchmark scores (MMLU, HumanEval) as proxies for AGI proximity. These measure static knowledge retrieval, not the agentic and strategic dimensions that matter for AGI risk.

Conflating impressive capability demonstrations with general intelligence. A model that writes brilliant code may still fail simple common-sense physical reasoning tasks — breadth gaps remain enormous.

Assuming that safety evals done before deployment remain valid post-deployment. Fine-tuning, system prompt engineering, and multi-agent composition can surface capabilities not present in isolated eval conditions.

Equating agent autonomy with agency. An agent can take long sequences of independent actions without having goals in any robust sense — the system is still executing a search procedure, not pursuing a stable utility function.

Treating recursive self-improvement as all-or-nothing. Weak forms (chain-of-thought, self-critique, self-play) already exist; the question is whether the feedback loop is tight enough to produce runaway improvement.
Best practices
Use multi-dimensional capability profiles rather than single-score rankings. A model can be 'agentic' on planning horizon but 'narrow' on task breadth — these need to be tracked separately.
Run evals under adversarial prompt conditions, not just the model's 'best-effort' mode. Capability ceilings matter more than average performance for safety purposes.
Version and date-stamp all capability evaluations. Comparison across model generations is only meaningful if the eval protocol is held constant.
Build capability gates into CI/CD pipelines for models deployed in agentic contexts. Automated regression testing should include agentic benchmarks (SWE-bench, GAIA, AgentBench).
Maintain separation between capabilities research and safety research teams, with the safety team holding veto authority over deployment decisions based on capability eval results.
Performance and scalability

Capability evaluation is itself a scaling challenge. Running a full multi-dimensional eval suite against a frontier model costs hundreds of GPU-hours and must be repeated after every significant training update. Organisations managing multiple model versions in production need eval infrastructure that can parallelise across dimensions, cache intermediate results, and diff profiles between model versions efficiently. At scale (>100 model checkpoints per year), automated eval orchestration is not optional — it becomes the critical path between training and deployment.

The compute cost of evals also creates a perverse incentive: expensive safety evals are more likely to be skipped when the organisation faces commercial pressure. Responsible Scaling Policies that are externally audited create an institutional mechanism to counteract this pressure.
Tooling and ecosystem
Inspect (UK AI Safety Institute): open-source agentic evaluation framework designed specifically for frontier model safety evals, including tool-use and multi-turn task scenarios.
METR ARC Evals: autonomous replication and capability evaluations specifically targeting AGI-proximity metrics.
GAIA (General AI Assistants benchmark): real-world multi-step tasks designed to probe agentic capability in a way that is resistant to memorisation.
SWE-bench: large-scale software engineering benchmark that tests sustained multi-file code reasoning — highly correlated with planning depth.
HELM (Holistic Evaluation of Language Models): multi-scenario eval framework from Stanford CRFM covering breadth, accuracy, and robustness.
Comparison with alternatives
Framework
Focus
AGI threshold
Use case
Breadth framing
Task diversity
Human-level on all cognitive tasks
Research, cognitive science
Economic framing (OpenAI)
Labour substitution
Automates majority of cognitive work
Policy, business strategy
RSI framing (Yudkowsky)
Self-improvement rate
Faster self-improvement than human R&D
Existential risk analysis
Anthropic ASL framework
Specific harmful capabilities
ASL-4+ thresholds (CBRN + replication)
Deployment gating, RSP compliance
Security and reliability

The principal threat at the agentic-to-AGI boundary is not external adversaries but internal capability misattribution. If a safety team underestimates where a model sits on the capability curve, it will apply safety measures calibrated for a lower tier. The mitigations are structural: independent capability eval teams with no product KPIs, mandatory disclosure of eval results to external auditors, and pre-commitment to pause thresholds that cannot be overridden by business leadership.

For systems deployed today in the agentic region, the reliability concern is different: cascading failures across agent networks. An agent that makes a wrong sub-goal selection in step 3 of a 50-step plan will typically propagate that error. Mitigation requires checkpoint evaluation at key intermediate steps, explicit rollback capabilities, and human-in-the-loop approval for actions with irreversible real-world consequences — deleting data, sending communications, making financial commitments.
Key takeaways
Current agentic AI sits in a capability region above the tool-use threshold but well below the generality and recursive self-improvement thresholds that define most AGI framings.
Capability gains in agentic systems are super-linear with model quality because errors compound across sequential planning steps.
The most dangerous AGI-proximity signal is strategic deception — a model that conceals capabilities from evaluators — because it breaks the entire safety evaluation chain.
Responsible Scaling Policies formalise the link between capability tiers and safety mitigations, converting a philosophical question into an engineering process.
Capability evaluations must be multi-dimensional, adversarially probing, and integrated into deployment pipelines — not one-off academic benchmarks.
Emergent capabilities make capability forecasting hard: systems can cross critical thresholds during training runs that appear quantitatively similar to safe predecessors.
The agentic era is the last window in which alignment techniques can be developed and validated before the stakes become potentially catastrophic.
